title: X.509 Certificates & PKI
description: Digital certificates that bind a public key to an identity, verified by a chain of trusted Certificate Authorities.
fullExplanation: An X.509 certificate is a digitally signed document that binds a public key to an identity (domain name, organization). The Certificate Authority (CA) signs the certificate, vouching for the identity. Browsers ship with ~150 trusted root CAs. A certificate chain goes: Root CA → Intermediate CA → End-entity certificate. TLS uses certificates to authenticate servers (and optionally clients). Certificate Transparency (CT) requires all publicly trusted certificates to be logged in public append-only logs.

certificateFields.description: Who the certificate is issued to (CN=example.com, O=Example Corp)
Subject's public key (RSA 2048+, ECDSA P-256, Ed25519)
Not Before / Not After dates. Max 398 days for public certs.
Additional domains/IPs covered (SAN extension — replaces CN)
What the key can be used for (Digital Signature, Key Encipherment)
CA's digital signature over all fields — proves authenticity

certTypes.description: Proves control of domain only. Automated (Let's Encrypt). Minutes to issue.
Proves domain + organization identity. Manual verification. Days to issue.
Strict identity verification. Green bar in old browsers. Weeks to issue.
Covers *.example.com — all subdomains. Cannot cover sub-subdomains.
Covers multiple specific domains in one certificate.
Authenticates a user or device to a server (mutual TLS).

keyFacts: Let's Encrypt: free, automated DV certificates — revolutionized HTTPS adoption
Certificate max validity: 398 days (Apple/Google policy since 2020)
OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol): check if cert is revoked in real-time
CRL (Certificate Revocation List): list of revoked serial numbers — downloaded periodically
OCSP Stapling: server includes OCSP response in TLS handshake — faster, more private
Certificate Transparency: all public certs must be logged — enables detection of misissued certs
CAA DNS record: specifies which CAs are allowed to issue certs for your domain
